items.stem: What does the speaker say teachers have been helping students with?

Narrator: Listen to a school principal speaking to the teaching staff.
Principal: Good afternoon, everyone. I called this meeting because I want to discuss a new project with you: the creation of a writing center.
As you know, a lot of our students are having trouble with writing and need extra help. I know many of you have stayed after class to help your students with essays and other writing assignments, and I really appreciate your efforts.
But now, however, we're going to do this in a coordinated manner. By creating a writing center, we'll be able to consistently help more students. I feel that the center will offer an invaluable service to the school community. Not only will it benefit our students, but parents will be happy too!
Now, there are many students who require assistance, but we don't want students coming in just expecting us to correct their mistakes or rewrite their papers. We need to make it clear that the center will not be an editing service.
Our primary goal should be to help students help themselves. In other words, we should strive to teach students how to build self-editing skills, you know, revise their own work.
We do face a couple of challenges. First, the room for the center will not be completely ready until the end of next month, so there may be some minor work going on during tutoring sessions.
And more importantly, we're going to need writing tutors, lots of them. Any volunteers? If you're willing, let me know as soon as possible.

items.stem: What is the main topic of the lecture?
options: The historical consequences of a major technological invention.
items.transcript:
Narrator: Listen to a history professor giving a lecture.
Professor: Let's get started. Today we're exploring the impact of the printing press during the Renaissance period. Prior to the mid-1400s, books were meticulously copied by hand, usually by monks. This made them incredibly rare, fragile, and astronomically expensive. Only the wealthiest individuals and institutions could afford to own a single manuscript. However, Johannes Gutenberg's introduction of movable type technology changed the landscape entirely. Suddenly, literature, scientific texts, and political pamphlets could be mass-produced at a fraction of the cost. This technological leap wasn't just about making books cheaper; it fundamentally altered European society. For the first time, information wasn't restricted to a small, elite group. Ideas began to spread rapidly across borders, fueling a massive increase in literacy rates among the middle class. People began to read and interpret texts for themselves, which ultimately paved the way for major scientific and cultural revolutions. Of course, this rapid spread of information wasn't welcomed by everyone. Various political and religious authorities saw this unregulated flow of ideas as a direct threat to their power and attempted to implement strict censorship laws. But the cat was already out of the bag. The printing press had ignited a thirst for knowledge that could not be suppressed. Next week, we’ll dive deeper into how this specific development influenced the scientific revolution. Please make sure you read the assigned chapter on early censorship before Tuesday's class, as there will be a short quiz.

While nothing has been severely critical, management takes these incidents very seriously, and we need to nip this trend in the bud before something major occurs. Today, we are focusing specifically on the proper protocols for operating the new heavy lifting machinery in warehouse section C. The new forklifts are much more powerful than our older models, and they have a significantly wider turning radius. This means you must maintain a larger clear zone when operating them. You’ll notice we’ve painted new yellow boundary lines on the floor; you must never cross these lines on foot while a forklift is in motion. Additionally, the pre-shift inspection checklist has been updated. Before you even turn the key, you are now required to digitally log the tire pressure and hydraulic fluid levels using the tablets mounted on the dashboard. I understand that this adds an extra five minutes to your morning routine, but cutting corners on safety checks is exactly what leads to accidents. We are going to head down to the warehouse now for a live demonstration. I expect everyone to pay close attention and ask questions if anything is unclear. Let's head out. Put your safety goggles on before entering the active zone.

What is the main point of the lecture?
options: Human memory is not a perfect record and can be easily changed.
items.transcript:
Narrator: Listen to a psychology instructor giving a lecture.
Instructor: Welcome back to Intro to Cognitive Psychology. Today, we’re going to discuss the fascinating phenomenon of memory distortion, specifically how our brains can create false memories. Many people believe that human memory works like a video camera, accurately recording and storing events exactly as they happened. However, decades of research have proven that memory is actually reconstructive. Every time you recall a past event, your brain actively pieces it back together, and during this process, the memory can be altered by new information, emotions, or even suggestive questions. A famous study in the 1970s demonstrated this perfectly. Participants watched a video of a minor car accident. Afterward, half the group was asked how fast the cars were going when they "hit" each other, while the other half was asked how fast they were going when they "smashed" into each other. The group that heard the word "smashed" estimated significantly higher speeds and even falsely remembered seeing broken glass in the video. This shows how easily external factors can contaminate our recollection. It’s a sobering thought, especially when you consider how heavily the legal system relies on eyewitness testimony. We tend to take our memories for granted, but they are far more fragile than we realize. For your upcoming essay, you'll be looking into real-world court cases where false memories played a critical role. I've uploaded a list of approved case studies to the student portal for you to choose from.

passages.title: The Philadelphia Waterworks
passages.text:
Philadelphia was the first capital of the United States. Despite being the nation's largest and wealthiest city, it relied on an inadequate assortment of wells, cisterns, and springs for water. To address this problem, the city developed the country's first municipal water supply.
The Philadelphia Waterworks was constructed along the Schuylkill River in 1801. It was an engineering marvel. Using the newly invented steam engine, water was pumped from the river to a reservoir on a hill. It flowed down throughout the city via water mains made from hollow logs. However, the steam engines soon proved too costly to operate, and were replaced by traditional waterwheels.
The dependable water supply fostered tremendous population growth. Unfortunately, this threatened the water supply since the river was used to dispose of an increasing amount of waste. To protect it from pollution, in 1844 the city began to acquire all the land that drained into the river and created the world's largest urban park.
This park and the waterworks quickly became a major tourist attraction. The blend of nature and technology was a reflection of nineteenth-century ideals: improving humanity through a combination of rural tranquility and technological progress; a landscaped "middle ground" between the sophistication of the city and the wholesomeness of the country. There was regular transportation from the city to the waterworks. Hotels and restaurants multiplied. It became the most reproduced scene of Philadelphia in the travel literature that was popular at the time.
The popularity of the waterworks would not last forever though. By 1872, because of population growth, new ways of drawing greater quantities of water from the river were needed. This meant the introduction of French-made turbines, which were effective but had no appeal for tourists. In the end, pollution of the river led to the closing of the waterworks in 1909.

passages.title: This passage is about the sleeping habits of marine mammals.
passages.text:
For most terrestrial animals, sleep involves a period of unconsciousness where the brain rests and the body remains still. However, for marine mammals like dolphins and whales, this type of sleep would be fatal. Because they are conscious breathers—meaning they must actively decide to take a breath—losing complete consciousness underwater would cause them to drown. Furthermore, a deep sleep would leave them highly vulnerable to predators in the open ocean.
To solve this evolutionary problem, dolphins utilize a fascinating biological adaptation known as unihemispheric slow-wave sleep (USWS). During USWS, a dolphin shuts down only one hemisphere of its brain at a time. The opposite eye remains open, allowing the animal to monitor its environment for threats, stay close to its pod, and periodically swim to the surface for air. After a couple of hours, the dolphin switches sides, resting the other half of its brain.
This unique capability ensures that dolphins can achieve the restorative benefits of sleep without ever truly letting their guard down. Recent studies have even shown that dolphins can maintain constant acoustic vigilance, continuously using echolocation to scan their surroundings for days on end without showing any signs of sleep deprivation.

Principal: Good afternoon, everyone. I called this meeting because I want to discuss a new project with you: the creation of a writing center.

passages.title: This passage is about working conditions.
passages.text:
Work culture in the UK and the United States has been evolving over the last few years, with several longstanding traditions being reassessed. Among them is the reconsideration of the five-day workweek, with numerous companies in these regions now trialing a four-day week.
Initially, some business leaders worried this change would negatively affect employee performance. However, studies show companies that have made such a change have increased productivity and revenue. In the five-day workweek, employees often suffer from fatigue, with office workers unable to focus on work-related tasks, and instead spending time on social media or personal matters. By working Monday through Thursday, studies found, employees were more focused, equaling or exceeding their five-day output. Management, too, worked to streamline processes, reducing meetings to increase productivity.
In these trials, workers still received the same salary, and by a wide margin, they wanted to retain this new system, as they found the extra day off allowed them to spend more time on recreation—almost no workers used the time to take on additional employment. Workers reported they would expect additional compensation if forced to return to the previous schedule.
Another recent change is the introduction of unlimited vacation days, which allows employees to take any number of days off a year, as long as their tasks are completed. While some approved of this system, one study found that workers felt more stress and were less likely to take days off without established norms for how much time off was acceptable. Many workers did not want to appear to be taking advantage of unlimited time off, so they ended up working more than their counterparts with traditional leave allowances.
With telecommuting more common and collaboration across time zones often necessary, the workplace continues to change. What the average office will look like a decade from now—or if most companies will even have them—remains to be seen.

passages.title: This passage is about the history of the modern workweek.
passages.text:
Today, the two-day weekend is a standard part of the global work culture, but it is a relatively recent invention. During the Industrial Revolution in the 19th century, factory workers in Europe and the United States routinely worked six or even seven days a week, often for 10 to 14 hours a day. The concept of having designated days off for leisure or family was virtually nonexistent for the working class, leading to widespread exhaustion and labor strikes.
The shift toward the modern weekend began in the early 20th century, largely championed by an unexpected figure: industrialist Henry Ford. In 1926, Ford instituted a five-day, 40-hour workweek for his automotive factory employees without reducing their pay. While labor unions had been fighting for shorter hours for decades, Ford’s motivation was primarily economic rather than strictly humanitarian. He realized that if his workers were constantly in the factory, they would never have the free time to drive—or buy—the very cars they were manufacturing.
Ford's policy proved to be highly successful, resulting in increased productivity and lower employee turnover. Recognizing these benefits, other major companies soon followed suit. Eventually, the movement gained enough momentum that governments began to codify it into law. In 1938, the United States passed the Fair Labor Standards Act, which officially established a maximum 40-hour workweek, cementing the two-day weekend as a permanent fixture of modern life.

Narrator: Listen to a school principal speaking to the teaching staff.
Principal: Good afternoon, everyone. I called this meeting because I want to discuss a new project with you: the creation of a writing center.
As you know, a lot of our students are having trouble with writing and need extra help. I know many of you have stayed after class to help your students with essays and other writing assignments, and I really appreciate your efforts.
But now, however, we're going to do this in a coordinated manner. By creating a writing center, we'll be able to consistently help more students. I feel that the center will offer an invaluable service to the school community. Not only will it benefit our students, but parents will be happy too!
Now, there are many students who require assistance, but we don't want students coming in just expecting us to correct their mistakes or rewrite their papers. We need to make it clear that the center will not be an editing service.
Our primary goal should be to help students help themselves. In other words, we should strive to teach students how to build self-editing skills, you know, revise their own work.
We do face a couple of challenges. First, the room for the center will not be completely ready until the end of next month, so there may be some minor work going on during tutoring sessions.
And more importantly, we're going to need writing tutors, lots of them. Any volunteers? If you're willing, let me know as soon as possible.

Narrator: Listen to a guide speaking to visitors at a space center.
Guide: Welcome to the National Space Exploration Center. We are currently standing in the command module exhibit, which houses the actual capsules used during the Apollo missions in the 1960s and 70s. As you look at these tiny, cramped spaces, it's hard to fathom that three grown men lived inside them for over a week while traveling to the moon. The technology you see here might look incredibly outdated to us now—in fact, the smartphone in your pocket has significantly more computing power than the computers that guided these astronauts through space. But at the time, this was the absolute cutting edge of human innovation. Now, I want to draw your attention to the heat shield on the bottom of the capsule right behind me. You can see the heavy scorching and burn marks. That damage is from re-entering the Earth's atmosphere at over twenty-four thousand miles per hour, where temperatures reached almost five thousand degrees. The engineering required to protect the crew from that kind of extreme heat is simply mind-boggling. We're going to move on to the lunar rover display next, but before we do, please make sure you keep your children close by. The lighting in the next hallway is quite dim to protect the vintage spacesuits on display, and it’s very easy to lose your bearings and get separated from your group. Follow me this way, please, and watch your step.

items.transcript:
Narrator: Listen to an environmental scientist speaking to city officials.
Scientist: Thank you for inviting me to speak today. I want to discuss a highly effective strategy for combating the urban heat island effect: the implementation of green roofs. As our city continues to expand, we are paving over natural landscapes with concrete and asphalt, materials that absorb and trap the sun's heat. This not only makes our downtown area uncomfortably hot during the summer but also significantly increases air conditioning costs and energy consumption. By installing green roofs—which essentially means covering the roof of a building with vegetation and soil—we can create a natural cooling system. The plants absorb the sunlight and release moisture into the air, which lowers the surrounding temperature. Furthermore, these rooftop gardens act as giant sponges during heavy rainstorms. They absorb a massive amount of rainwater, which helps prevent our city's aging sewer system from overflowing and flooding the streets. Now, I understand that the initial installation costs can be a bitter pill to swallow for many property owners. The structural reinforcements and specialized waterproofing membranes are certainly not cheap. However, we must look at the big picture. The long-term savings in energy bills and roof maintenance, combined with the obvious environmental benefits, make this a remarkably sound investment for the future of our urban environment. I strongly urge the city council to consider implementing tax incentives or grants to encourage local developers and businesses to adopt this innovative technology. We really need to act now before the extreme summer heat becomes completely unbearable for our residents.

I know some of the senior staff members are feeling a bit hesitant about abandoning the classic look that built our initial success. However, in today's fiercely competitive market, if we don't adapt and evolve, we will inevitably be left behind by our more agile competitors. We simply cannot rest on our laurels. The design department has already finalized the new primary logo, and I have printed copies for everyone to review. Please take a close look at the brand guidelines packet in front of you. passages.title: This passage is about a location in the Arctic.
passages.text:
Eight hundred kilometers north of the Norwegian mainland lies the tiny archipelago of Svalbard. Well within the Arctic Circle, Svalbard is the northernmost continually inhabited settlement in the world. It also boasts the world's northernmost institute of higher education. But Svalbard is unique for another reason: it is home to the world's Global Seed Vault.
Although dozens of countries have national seed banks, the vault in Svalbard was designed to be the ultimate backup for them all. Its remote location would keep it relatively safe from any wide-scale military engagements that might someday occur, and its climate provides an ideal storage solution for keeping seeds viable long into the future. Due to its extreme northern location, Svalbard rarely gets above 15 degrees Celsius during polar summer, where the sun can be seen almost 24 hours a day. During the winter, temperatures are as low as -40 degrees Celsius. As a result, the land historically has been in a state of permafrost, meaning that the soil always stays frozen. In deep freeze, the Global Seed Vault holds the seeds of more than 930,000 types of crops, including 140,000 kinds of wheat.
It is easy to understand the critical importance of a Global Seed Vault in the face of a catastrophe, but the main threat that the Global Seed Vault seeks to mitigate is actually much more mundane. Large-scale industrial agriculture consists of monocrops—one crop variety grown from cloned seeds. As a result, a certain type of pest or illness could wipe out the entire crop. In contrast, farmers used to grow dozens of crop varieties routinely, so they always had backup options if one variety failed. In Taiwan, farmers used to grow over 1,700 types of rice, but now the vast majority of rice paddies only grow three varieties. The Global Seed Vault protects the genetic variation of the world's crops, ensuring that valuable diversity isn't lost for future generations.

passages.title: This passage is about a new agricultural method.
passages.text:
As the global population continues to expand, traditional farming is facing an unprecedented crisis. Arable land is becoming scarce, fresh water is rapidly depleting, and unpredictable weather patterns caused by climate change frequently destroy crops. In response to these challenges, agricultural engineers have developed a method called vertical farming. Instead of growing crops horizontally in expansive outdoor fields, vertical farming involves cultivating plants in stacked layers within controlled indoor environments, such as abandoned warehouses or specially designed skyscrapers.
This innovative method relies heavily on technology. Rather than using soil, vertical farms typically utilize hydroponics or aeroponics, delivering water and essential nutrients directly to the plants' roots. Sunlight is replaced by specialized LED lighting systems that emit specific wavelengths optimized for photosynthesis. Because the environment is entirely enclosed, vertical farms do not require pesticides or herbicides, and they can produce yields year-round regardless of the season or external weather conditions.
Despite its obvious advantages, vertical farming is not without its critics. The most significant drawback is the massive energy consumption required to power the artificial lighting and climate control systems 24 hours a day. Until renewable energy sources become cheaper and more efficient, the high operational costs mean that vertical farms currently focus mostly on high-profit crops like leafy greens and herbs, rather than staple crops like wheat or corn that the world needs in massive quantities.

passages.title: This passage is about changes in consumer payment methods.
passages.text:
Over the past decade, a growing number of countries have begun moving toward a cashless society, a financial environment where physical money—banknotes and coins—is replaced by digital transactions. Driven by the widespread adoption of smartphones and contactless payment technologies, consumers are increasingly using credit cards, mobile wallets, and banking apps to make everyday purchases. In countries like Sweden, cash is now used in less than ten percent of all transactions, prompting debates about the future of physical money.
The shift toward digital payments offers several distinct advantages. For businesses, handling digital money is faster and more efficient, eliminating the need to count cash at the end of the day or physically transport it to a bank. Furthermore, proponents argue that a cashless system significantly reduces certain types of crime, such as bank robberies and tax evasion, since digital transactions leave a permanent and traceable electronic record.
Despite these benefits, experts warn that entirely eliminating cash could have serious societal consequences. A primary concern is the exclusion of marginalized populations. The elderly, low-income individuals, and those living in rural areas often lack access to reliable internet, smartphones, or traditional bank accounts. If physical money becomes obsolete, these groups could be effectively shut out of the economy. Additionally, privacy advocates worry that a fully cashless society would allow corporations and governments to monitor every purchase a person makes, leading to an unprecedented loss of financial privacy.

passages.title: This passage is about a unique survival strategy in nature.
passages.text:
In the dense rainforests of Central and South America, the glass frog has developed a fascinating method of camouflage that puzzles many researchers. Unlike most animals that use colors to blend into their surroundings, the glass frog possesses translucent skin on its underbelly, making its internal organs, including its heart and liver, clearly visible. This transparency serves a vital purpose: it helps the frog disappear against the bright green leaves where it rests during the day, protecting it from sharp-eyed predators like snakes and birds.
The secret to this "disappearing act" was recently discovered to be more than just skin deep. Scientists found that while the glass frog sleeps, it redirects nearly 90 percent of its red blood cells into its liver. By hiding the blood cells, which are opaque and reflect light, the frog increases its transparency level significantly. This physiological process is remarkable because, in most other vertebrates, such a high concentration of red blood cells would cause dangerous blood clots. Somehow, the glass frog’s body manages to pack these cells together without any negative health effects.
Understanding how these frogs manage their blood flow could have significant implications for human medicine. Researchers are currently studying the chemical signals the frogs use to prevent clotting. If scientists can replicate this process, it might lead to new treatments for cardiovascular diseases in humans, specifically for preventing strokes or heart attacks caused by internal blockages. This small amphibian, once just a biological curiosity, may now hold the key to a major medical breakthrough.

passages.title: This passage is about a psychological phenomenon.
passages.text:
Have you ever walked into a room and completely forgotten why you went there? This common experience is known among psychologists as the "Doorway Effect." It suggests that the simple act of passing through a doorway triggers a mental "reset" in the brain. According to this theory, our brains categorize information based on our current environment. When we move from one room to another, the brain archives the thoughts related to the previous room to make space for the new information it expects to encounter in the next one.
In a series of studies, participants were asked to perform simple memory tasks while moving between rooms. The results consistently showed that people were more likely to forget items or objectives after walking through a door compared to walking the same distance within a single room. Researchers believe this happens because the brain perceives the doorway as an "event boundary." Just like a new chapter in a book, the doorway signals that one episode has ended and another has begun, causing the brain to prioritize the current setting over past intentions.
While the Doorway Effect can be frustrating, it actually highlights the efficiency of human memory. It shows that our brains are designed to focus on the "here and now," which was a crucial survival trait for our ancestors. However, in our modern, multi-room lives, this mechanism can lead to minor inconveniences. To combat this, experts suggest that mentally repeating your goal as you cross the threshold can help maintain the focus. By doing so, you essentially "force" the brain to keep that specific piece of information active across the event boundary.

passages.title: This passage is about the history of the Rosetta Stone.
passages.text:
For centuries, the ancient Egyptian hieroglyphic script was a mystery that no scholar could solve. The ability to read these complex symbols had been lost for over a thousand years, leaving the history of a great civilization locked away. This changed in 1799 when French soldiers discovered a large stone slab near the town of Rashid, or Rosetta. The stone was unique because it featured the same royal decree written in three different scripts: Hieroglyphic, Demotic (a later Egyptian script), and Ancient Greek.
Because scholars could still read Ancient Greek, the Rosetta Stone provided the essential "key" to unlocking the other two languages. By comparing the Greek text to the hieroglyphs, researchers began to identify patterns and names. However, the process was not immediate. It took twenty years of intense study before a young Frenchman named Jean-François Champollion finally cracked the code. He realized that hieroglyphs were not just pictures representing objects, but also symbols representing sounds, much like the letters in a modern alphabet.
The successful translation of the Rosetta Stone revolutionized the field of Egyptology. Suddenly, thousands of inscriptions on temple walls and papyrus scrolls became readable, providing a first-hand account of Egyptian politics, religion, and daily life. The stone itself is now housed in the British Museum, where it remains one of the most visited artifacts in history. It stands as a testament to the power of language and the persistence of human curiosity in uncovering the secrets of the past.
